summary:
Ain Mallaha (also known as Eynan) is a Natufian and early Neolithic site located near Lake Huleh in northern Israel, occupied from approximately 12,000 to 8,000 BCE. It is one of the best-documented Natufian settlements and is notable for providing some of the earliest known evidence of complex sedentary or semi-sedentary forager communities transitioning toward food production. The site features substantial stone-built structures, extensive burials, and evidence of a highly productive mixed subsistence economy based on intensive exploitation of wild cereals, fish, and game.

Among the most celebrated finds at Ain Mallaha is a burial dating to approximately 12,000 years ago in which a human skeleton was interred with a puppy — one of the earliest known examples of human-dog companionship in the archaeological record. This burial, and others at the site, suggest that the Natufian inhabitants of Ain Mallaha had complex social rituals and possibly strong emotional bonds with domestic or proto-domestic animals. The rich array of burials at the site also provides evidence of social differentiation, with some individuals receiving more elaborate burial treatment than others.

By the period covered in this era (8000–7500 BCE), Ain Mallaha had passed its Natufian peak but continued to function as part of the landscape in which early farming communities of the Levantine Neolithic developed. The site's long occupation history makes it a key reference point for understanding the gradual transition from intensive foraging to full-scale agriculture in the southern Levant.
